summary: I was born in Tunis, Tunisia in 1959, just a few years after the country gained independence. Tunis is where my family lived, and it was the place I always returned to despite my travels. It holds a special place in my heart as the city of my childhood and early education.

Tunisia is my homeland, where I was born and raised after it gained independence from the French colonial empire in 1956. Despite the tough economic conditions there, I always felt a strong connection to my country and family. I returned there multiple times between my travels abroad, and eventually, in 2007, I married my high school sweetheart in Tunisia before settling permanently in the United States.

Tunisia was part of the French colonial empire until 1956, just three years before I was born. The legacy of French colonization influenced the political and economic landscape I grew up in. It was after independence from the French colonial empire that my family and I experienced the challenges of building a new nation.

In 1981, I moved to Paris, France on a student visa to study mechanical engineering. Paris was my first destination abroad, and I spent two years there before returning to Tunisia to complete my studies. It was an important step in my educational journey and my first real experience living outside Tunisia.

France was where I first went to study abroad in Paris during the early 1980s. The country represented opportunity and education for me, especially as I pursued mechanical engineering. My connection to France also ties back to Tunisia's history under the French colonial empire, which shaped much of my early life.

In 1985, I traveled to Brussels, Belgium on a tourist visa to join my friend Mohsan and find work. I stayed there for over two years, trying to improve my economic situation. Brussels was a significant chapter in my life as it was my first experience working abroad.

Belgium became my home for a couple of years in the mid-1980s when I lived in Brussels. I went there hoping to find better economic opportunities and stayed working with the help of my friend. It was a challenging but important period that helped me gain international experience.

In 1987, I moved to Norway after receiving an invitation from my brother Mohamed, who lived in Oslo. I stayed there for two years and even met a Norwegian woman I planned to marry. However, the language barrier, weather, and cultural differences made it hard for me to adapt, so I returned to Tunisia in 1989.

Oslo was where I lived during my two-year stay in Norway starting in 1987. My brother Mohamed was already a resident there and invited me to join him. Although I hoped to settle down in Oslo, the difficulties I faced led me to leave the city and return home.

I went to Canada in 1990 on a tourist visa and submitted an immigration application, hoping to start a new life there. I stayed and worked for about a year but faced setbacks when my friends, whom I trusted to handle my immigration process, betrayed me. This disappointment delayed my plans and eventually led me to move to the United States.

The United States became a major part of my life starting in 1991 when I first arrived in Berkeley, California. I stayed with my friend Lotfi and later moved to San Francisco, where I worked and lived for several years. After many travels back and forth, I finally made the U.S. my permanent home with my wife and two daughters.

Berkeley, California was where I first arrived in the United States in 1991, staying with my friend Lotfi for six months. Many years later, I returned to live there permanently with my wife and daughters, just a short distance from my original residence. Berkeley has become my home after all my journeys across the world.

In 1995, I moved to San Francisco, California on a tourist visa and lived there for five years. It was in San Francisco that I met and married an American woman, although our marriage ended after two years. The city was an important place for work and life during that period of transition.
